content.p: Find the interval of convergence for \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \sum_{ n= {{start}} }^\infty {{summand}} .

outtro.p: We begin with finding the radius of convergence R. By letting \renewcommand{\log}{\ln}\displaystyle b_n = {{summand}}, we have that \displaystyle \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} b_{n+1} = {{Bnp1}}. We wish to find the values of x where \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \lim_{n\to\infty} \left| {{Bnp1}} \cdot {{Bnr}} \right| < 1 which occurs precisely when \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} |{{xma}}| \lim_{n\to\infty} \left( {{aRatio}} \right) < 1. By evaluating the limit, we see that this occurs when \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} |{{xma}}| < {{R}} or when \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle {{Lpoint}} < x < {{Rpoint}}.
When \displaystyle x={{Rpoint}}, we obtain the series \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \sum_{n = {{start}} }^\infty {{Rtest}} which {{Rtext}} (the student should verify this).
When \displaystyle x={{Lpoint}}, consider the limit \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \sum_{n = {{start}} }^\infty {{Ltest}} which {{Ltext}} (the student should verify this).
Thus the interval of convergence is \renewcommand{\log}{\ln}\displaystyle {{Lsymbol}} {{Lpoint}}, {{Rpoint}} {{Rsymbol}} .

We begin with finding the radius of convergence R. By letting \renewcommand{\log}{\ln}\displaystyle b_n = {{summand}}, we have that \displaystyle \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} b_{n+1} = {{Bnp1}}. We wish to find the values of x where \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \lim_{n\to\infty} \left| {{Bnp1}} \cdot {{Bnr}} \right| < 1 which occurs precisely when \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} |{{xma}}| \lim_{n\to\infty} \left( {{aRatio}} \right) < 1. By evaluating the limit, we see that this occurs when \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} |{{xma}}|\cdot 0 < 1. This inequality holds for every real number. Thus the interval of convergence is \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle (-\infty, \infty).
